# Top 5 posts from r/MovieSuggestions day

## 1. What are some good Con Movies?

**Score:** 14
**Body:** I have already watched the following 

The Sting

American Hustle

The Grifters 

Catch Me if you Can

Dirty Rotten Scoundrels

All Oceans Eleven franchise Movies.

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Jackie Brown
2. Paper Moon
3. House of Games (1987) 
A psychiatrist comes to the aid of a compulsive gambler and is led by a smooth-talking grifter into the shadowy but compelling world of stings, scams, and con men.

Matchstick Men (2003)
A phobic con artist and his protégé are on the verge of pulling off a lucrative swindle when the former's teenage daughter arrives unexpectedly.
4. Snatch
5. The Spanish Prisoner (1997) and Heist (2001) both directed by David Mamet
6. Con-Air
7. Inside man
8. Sneakers

Heat

Goodfellas

Usual Suspects 

Thief

Live and Die in LA

Reservoir Dogs
9. Diggstown is a fun one
10. The Talented Mr Ripley  - 1999

## 2. Requesting lighthearted, fun movies. Please read on.

**Score:** 10
**Body:** Just watched David Lynch's Wild at Heart and Blue Velvet back to back. Now my own heart is going through all stages that Sandy experienced in the two films. Thankfully, both had fairly happy endings (WaH's being weirdly forced). 

I enjoyed the rollercoaster rides. Now I'm craving for something that's simple and fun. Could be a comedy movie, but that's not a requirement. For some reason, I have never been able to laugh while watching a comedy movie.

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Best in Show (2000)
2. Have you tried The Grand Budapest Hotel? It's got that Wes Anderson whimsy that might hit different after Lynch's intensity. Not exactly laugh-out-loud funny but weirdly charming and visually satisfying

  
Alternatively, maybe something like Paddington or Hunt for the Wilderpeople if you want pure wholesome vibes
3. Hundreds of Beavers 

Dave Made A Maze 

Shaolin Soccer 

The Birdcage 

Kung Fu Hustle 

When Harry Met Sally
4. Shaun of the Dead, watching it right now
  - And follow it up with a bit of Hot Fuzz 👮‍♂️
5. The Secret Life of Walter Mitty (2013)  
  
Chef (2014)
6. Sing Street is my go-to recommendation for a fun movie, check it out.
7. Rat Race
8. Champions (2023) 

“A former minor-league basketball coach receives a court order to manage a team of players with intellectual disabilities. Despite his doubts, he soon realizes that together they can go further than they ever imagined.”
9. I just watched “Knives Out” on Amazon prime and loved it. 
I normally love documentaries, but wanted something lighthearted and was pleasantly surprised by this one.

## 3. Looking for movies about or that deal with memory/memories

**Score:** 9
**Body:** Like the title says, I’m looking for movies that are about or that deal with memory/memories. Any genre and any time period is fine. I’m very grateful to anyone who recommends, so thank you. 

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Memento

Eternal Sunshine of the Spotless Mind

Big Fish

Still Alice

Inception
  - Agree with each of these. Adding on:

Total Recall

Self/less

Life Itself (by Dan Fogelman)
2. Eternal Sunshine of the Spotless Mind is probably the obvious choice but it's genuinely incredible if you haven't seen it. Also check out Memento - that one will mess with your head in the best way possible
3. Regarding Henry with Harrison Ford as a severe business man who gets in a car accident resulting in brain trauma. It's a really good movie. I think Annette Bening is his wife.
4. The Father (2020)

Jacob’s Ladder (1990)

The Machinist (2004)

Blade Runner franchise
5. The Notebook.
6. Still Alice (2014)


The Father (2020)


Three of Us (2022)


Source Code (2011)
7. Sophie's Choice
8. Oh yes, also, 50 First Dates with Drew Barrymore and Adam Sandler. Worth seeing. I didn't have a clue when I went to see it, so if you go assuming it's a good comedy and a good love story, you'd be partly right.
9. Johnny Mnemonic

## 4. Looking for a movie that will keep me at the edge of seat or which will question my existence

**Score:** 10
**Body:** I have really liked movies like 

Shutter Island, Predestination (that made me question my existence) ,

Seven,Zodiac, Prisoners,Get out,Gone girl (that kept me at edge of seat) .

So any suggestions please. Any thriller genre is fine . I need to be  mindblown after I watch it.


Edit: Thanks in advance to everyone for ur suggestion I will definitely watch all of them ,i have unlimited internet.

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The Game (1997) follows wealthy, cold banker Nicholas Van Orton (Michael Douglas) who receives a mysterious birthday gift from his estranged brother(Sean Penn): a chance to play a personalized, real-life game run by Consumer Recreation Services (CRS). The game blurs the lines between reality and fiction, thrusting him into increasingly dangerous, elaborate scenarios that challenge his sanity and force him to confront his past, leaving him unable to trust anyone as the game seems intent on destroying him, all leading to a shocking reveal about the game's true purpose
  - Seriously love this movie. Hubby just suggested a rewatch this weekend. Highly recommend.
  - Alright sure 👍🏻
2. I keep a list of must see mindfuck movies, and one of must see psychological thriller movies. LMK if you’d like to see one or the other . Too hard to narrow down if you don’t know anybody so I can paste one or the other
3. Coherence (2013) will absolutely mess with your head - it's this low budget sci-fi thriller that starts as a dinner party and becomes pure psychological chaos. Also check out The Man from Earth if you want something that'll have you questioning reality for days
4. You named some of my favorite movies in your list; it looks like your taste is very similar to mine.

The Usual Suspects

Primal Fear

Mulholland Drive

L.A. Confidential

Memento

To Die For

Eyes Wide Shut

Interstellar = Life-changing

Doubt

One Battle After Another

Fight Club
 
12 Monkeys

Devil in a Blue Dress

Training Day

Death Proof

Bodies Bodies Bodies

Knives Out

Gone Baby Gone

Girl With The Dragon Tattoo
5. The Prestige - if you have not already seen it!

The Fountain - also tickles my existential ideology!
  - I have watched prestige will watch other one 👍🏻
6. Triangle
7. Don’t care what anyone says - Vanilla Sky is peak

## 5. Looking for films in the 50s/60s/70s/maybe 80s with classical historical settings or claymation monsters.

**Score:** 9
**Body:** 
Looking for films akin to Jason and the Argonauts (1963), Clash of the Titans (1981), Sinbad films (1958, 1974, 1977), The 10 Commandments (1956), Ben Hur (1959). Grew up appreciating the special effects of these films and overall I enjoy the aesthetics of older films that use the settings of classical era cultures. What other films would you recommend that share some of the aspects of these films? 

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. You're confusing "claymation" with "stop motion animation". Claymation is specifically the animation of clay (or plasticine) models. Like Wallace and Gromit. 

Jason and the Argonauts, the Sinbad movies, and Clash of the titans are examples of animated wire frame models by Ray Harryhausen.

Have you seen The Valley of Gwangi (1969)?
2. well...don't watch The Adventures of Mark Twain (1985) claymation movie.

also,...do you  have your heart set on 'Claymation'? or will any stop-motion based film work?  Such as Hidari (2023), a Japanese work using wood.
3. None of these have Classical Historical Settings, but:

Mysterious Island (1961)

First Men in the Moon (1964)

Jack the Giant Killer (1962)

That last is one more Gumby claymation level than Harryhausen stop-motion.
4. One of my favorites is Equinox (1970). Famously low budget but so entertaining. 
  - Basically a home movie made by a bunch of enthusiastic college kids, some of whom would soon go on to found ILM.
5. Anything by Ray Harryhausen, he was the animator behind *Jason and the Argonauts*, *The 7th Voyage of Sinbad, The Golden Voyage of Sinbad, Sinbad and the Eye of the Tiger*, as well as *Clash of the Titans* (his last film). Other films he made, that you might enjoy, are *One Million Years BC* (1966), and *The Valley of the Gwangi* (1969).

Similar adventure films from the '50s and '60s you might like are *20, 000 Leagues Under the Sea* (1954), *Journey to the Centre of the Earth* (1959), *The Time Machine* (1960), and *The Thief of Bagdad* (1940).
6. Army of Darkness came out in the early 90s but imo it feels like a movie from the 80s.
7. Mysterious Island
8. The Third Man (historical setting)
9. One Million Years B.C. (1966)

# Top 5 posts from r/moviecritic day

## 1. This was a very well-done body horror film

**Score:** 169
**Body:** Spoilers ahead of course. I'm a big fan of body horror in general like the Fly and the Thing, and I thought this was a wonderful take on the genre. And definitely reminded me of fusion-ha! on DragonBall Z, ha.


Finally saw it recently because it came out on streaming, and I was only vaguely aware of the premise of them getting stuck together. It was very well done as a slow burn. I like how the conjoining virus or whatever starts off psychological at first, and starts taking over their minds before it joins their bodies. The couples fights/banter between Brie and Franco is extremely believable because they're actually freaking married. The happy ending was just icing on the cake! Just give in to it! To compare it to another recent body horror film, find this movie much more rewatchable than the Substance. That's a one and done for me. 


My only questions are, why didn't Millie and Tim running to the hiker monster their first night in the sunken church cave? And more importantly, why didn't they finish fusing into a harmonious single person? Did they explain how they got stuck in that transition? 

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Can you clarify your first question in the last paragraph? The grammar is a little confusing like you’re missing a word?
  - When the main characters fell into the hole, why did they not encounter the partly merged couple that went missing. 

When later in the film, when Tim went back, he encountered them.
2. For the first question, Im not sure and wondered the same thing. They do hear some off shit down there the first night, though.

As for the second question, I believe one of the two conjoining hikers killed themselves during the merge. They had a knife in their head or something, haven't seen the movie since it's release.
3. Hooooooo did the very last scene make me cackle with laughter. The rest was pretty alright, but goddamn that last image of the movie made me laaaauuuugh.
  - Same! I was like what in the Elliot Page is this 😆😆
4. Why the hikers didn’t fully fuse, might have something to do with one of them being dead so the fusion couldn’t be completed? Sorry, I didn’t fully comprehend the whole reasoning
  - It s because they “fought” it va embracing it.
  - My read is that. They had started to merge and one of them thought if they ended their life it'd break the process so the other could live. But all that did was leave them merging with a dead body.
  - Because they didn't embrace the fusion.
5. Definitely. 2025 was a great year for horror and this definitely made it on my top 10 list overall

## 2. Unpopular opinion: Onward is a top 3 Pixar film

**Score:** 138
**Body:** I know Pixar has some great films, but as a young father and older brother this one hits really hard. I’ll admit it’s not their best (I think that has to be Toy Story and coco) but it may be my personal favorite after Toy Story. Had me balling by the end. 

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. It was good, but definitely not top three for Pixar
2. Underrated for sure
3. Underrated for sure. But not top 3 for me. Inside out, Wall-E, Ratatouille, Incredibles, and Coco are ahead in my book. Easily top 10, though.
4. I LOVED Onward - did not know it was going to impact me so deeply (sobs). What’s weird is I can never remember the name of this movie though
5. Not top 3 for me, but I grew up with 3 brothers, so it feels more special for me in that aspect.
6. I cried very hard watching this (am a grownup)
7. I... Agree with your unpopular opinion. Seriously. It's a really good one.
8. I agree it’s a very good one and it’s a shame not as many people have seen it.
9. I have a HUGE soft spot for this one cause I have 2 boys.  I can't say it's as good as some of the top Pixar stuff but I absolutely love it.
10. It is peak

## 3. I feel like Withnail & I and The Big Lebowski are the British and American versions of the same film.

**Score:** 24
**Body:** I’m so obsessed with this idea but I feel like I’m maybe the only one who thinks this??

There’s a plot, but the plot isn’t the main point of the film - the film actually revolves around the dynamic and the dialogue between the two main characters, two friends who have a curiously uneasy relationship. Many interesting side characters come and go. The plot is driven by an older, wealthy benefactor. The film itself is a feast of quotable lines in every scene - with a bunch of obvious first-time-you-watch jokes, but as you watch it repeatedly you come to appreciate the more subtle lines in every scene.

Both films are chronically nationally coded - W&I is \*very\* British, and TBL is \*very\* American. But they’re the very British/American interpretations of the same underlying film concept.

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. This is a really interesting take, and one I had not thought of! 

I can see:

- The elevation of shaggy dog story to central plot

- The elegant waster/life flaneur as tragicomic hero 

- The movie as iconic collection of quotable one-liners and bon mot material 

- Movie as an elegy for a particular life and milieu


- I think elsewhere, they differ: The Big Lebowski is about the quest (the title is someone other than the protagonists); Withnail & I is ultimately a study of the relationship and what happens when one of the pair grows and the other stays. 

But lots to think about here!
2. The Big Lebowski constantly subverts genre. It's a Raymond Chandler mystery, but there's no crime, no kidnap money and no detective. It's a story of American heroism, as signaled by Sam West's narration, without any heroism. It's a sports movie that never reaches the final. Jeff Bridges' character is at the center of all of this, "taking it easy for all of us sinners". His character's lack of drive is the rug that ties the movie together. 

I don't know Withnail & I well enough but I think that the central character who resists all expectations that he will aim at any objective that might drive the action is the common factor you're getting at.
3. Don’t just downvote you bastards - engage! Discuss it if you don’t agree!
  - These are nihilist Donny. These men are cowards.
    - Lolol. That cracked me up.
4. Agree, some brilliant structured insights here.

My take would simply be,

\* \*”Both films are about people drifting on the fringes of society”\*

It is an interesting angle to take for a film:

1. In contrast to so much society messaging “do this, do that“…

2. In contrast to The Hero’s Journey it’s about falling off the Quest…

3. Shows how life is unplugged and unstructured

With that said both films are very different:

1. Withnail and I is almost pure autobiography of the writer and director Bruce Robertson and his experiences of living down and out in late-60’s London. He is very candid who the characters are based on eg Uncle Monty. Because of this it works because it resonates with many young people going through this phase of wanting to live to the full while also trying to find a way forwards in life and the confusion of the two in this formative stage of life and of course the passing of it and the glowing memories of living richly amidst impoverishment.

2. The Big Lebowski is altogether a lot more artificial construction, a lot more surreal than bleak reality (Withnail‘s washed out grey of that period a massive post-war hangover) and a sequence of dreams and visions while wide awake in an absurd modern day reality. I think the comment describing “no hero” narration overlay points this out accurately. This probably reflects something to do with American culture and the Big American Dream and its hang-over?

Notably both stories deal with reality splintering from the main narratives as above often alongside substantial substance abuse perhaps in both cases as a form of self-medication and consolation for slipping off the so-called “golden path of life”?!

Hence the sympathy, humour and geniality and enduring enjoyment and cult film status worth repeat viewing each succeed in delivering.

Equally given the above, both must have been made with consummate skills to achieve this, a whole technical discussion below the above froth, as worthy of mention too if any cinematographers can do either film justice?

One final observation: Watching Withnail And I, younger than the main characters then the same age or phase of life then older than them is interesting too. Younger self, the sheer antics were rewarding, same age the dreams and aspirations of “making it” and older self the escape to the old country and simple good life, all take their turns in appealing more.

One criticism: You could argue both films glamourise this outcome or mode of life, but for purposes of telling an entertaining story that is an acceptable deficit to telling tall tales and increasing the sense of fun shared, so long as it is also appreciated, living simply and unglamorously (eg in the old cottage in the Lake District or Cambden Town in Withnail or owning a nice rug “that really pulled the room together”) is good enough, too, even unsung.

Uplifting to see how many people got a lot from both films, here.
  - Ah mate this is brilliant, you actually put way more effort into the analysis than I did 😅

Spot on. And you’ve also hit on a difference I hadn’t considered - you’re right, the characters in W&I are striving, they want to “make it”; the characters in TBL are settled, they’re content with their lot.
5. fwiw i agree
6. Damn OP, this is good. I'm an American who lived in London for 4 years. Watching W&I for the first time with British mates who had seen it countless times made me understand in ways I wouldn't have been able to otherwise. I never thought of the parallels to perhaps the movie I've watched more times than any other (Big Lebowski) but it all makes sense now.  

You deserve the finest wines available to humanity.
7. The Camberwell carrot works well in either film.

## 4. "I See You (2019)" is emblematic of a sad shift in narrative development in the last few decades

**Score:** 0
**Body:** I just watched I See You after being told that it is a masterful horror movie that will leave me on the edge my seat by 2 different people, and I found myself scratching my head.

Its not necessarily a terrible movie, but it falls flat on its face by not delivering on audience expectations and trying to jigsaw together narrative elements that seemingly someone expected to be impressive without a cohesive structure.

SPOILERS PAST THIS POINT:  
The twists can be summarized as   
\- you thought it was a supernatural movie but aha it was real people  
\- you thought it was it was a detective thriller but aha it was phroggers  
\- you thought it was the phroggers that were bad but aha it was the cop  
\- you thought it was a coincidence that the phroggers were there but aha it was planned

By the fourth "twist" I found myself more bored by the question of alright, what "twist" is coming next? At least the story is narratively consistent, which can't be said of many movies like it.

The cheating thread at the beginning of the movie is the only part that held any emotional weight, but the strained family dynamics are played so cheesily and then quickly abandoned and not brought up again.

It feels like the movie was designed around having great twists, and not telling a good story. One of those movies where netflix expects you to be on your phone so that every twist needs to be spelled out and theres no space for nuance. What I think proves this, is that the final twist, being that Alec was the cops victim who got away is played so heavy handedly, its as if it was revealed 4 times in a row. Any one of the 4 "reveals" at the end suffice to an audience that has paid attention, and then the pacing dies a quick death.

  
I question whether the suggestion that the movie was good came from inattentive viewing where the twists were noted down as reasons that the movie is good instead of genuine enjoyment. I've had both good and bad movies and shows suggested for reasons like "you won't believe the twists!" or "the scares are insane!"

SPOILERS FOR ATTACK ON TITAN AND HEREDITARY:

  
Two pieces of media I really enjoy!! 

However,  Attack on Titan was pitched to me and I regularly see online as being the show with the best twists, and you can never guess whats coming next. But the show goes out of its way to deliver on a carefully crafted narrative about power and immaturity, that explores the idea of handing a kid (Eren being like what 16? at the beginning) godlike power and him being unable to handle it. But audiences love the twists and thats what gets media coverage.

Hereditary was pitched as the scariest movie cuz I'll never see the jumpscares coming. But I loved it because Toni Collete's acting with the rest of the family delivered an amazing portrayal of inter-family dynamics with a perfectly executed payoff with the grandma's odd behavior being to summon Paimon. The beauty of that movie is in the family dynamics and how the perspectives of each family member shifts as the true story is revealed, and the tragedy of Peter's experience with it all.

Is it scary? Sure! Really scary! But simplifying it to the "scariest horror movie" does it such an injustice, and seems to lead directors to forego making works of art and instead create movies like I See You which execute "twists" and "scares" formulaically to sell to a streaming service.

I believe it could have been a good movie if its 1.5hr runtime was extended and explored parallels in the family's dynamic with the father hiding his true nature and Alec doing the same. Looking at how this affects Mindy and the father's son emotionally and how they reconcile that with the person they love would have added a weight to the movie that was entirely missing!

  
Alright, this was a bit of a dumb rant and the movie was fine, I just noticed a trend and decided to scream into the void. Do what you will with that

### Top 0 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)


## 5. Anyone else mad at The Housemaid casting??? (I.e. Sydney Sweeney)

**Score:** 0

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I thought she was good in the role.
2. Not in the slightest.
3. She was great!
I didn't see any problem with her acting... I even read the book and Sweeney perfectly captured the character! Sydney Sweeney has good performances... there are many actresses out there that everyone loves... but they deliver mediocre performances!
  - I think I stand corrected by this loyal Sweeney subreddit. I’m open to critique. I felt like Millie was snarkier in the book (internally). Didn’t see that developed in the movie
    - Forgive me if I was ignorant!
But I loved the book and I also liked the movie!
Sweeney was exactly as I thought she would be... I can't see another actress playing the role!
Besides... I'm tired of this stereotype (I'm not referring to you) that she only has breasts, she's not SPECTACULAR, but I easily liked her performances in Eden, Reality and now in The Housemaid!
But yes... Amanda Seyfried dominated the film with her masterful performance!
I believe that if anything went wrong, it was the direction!
4. No
5. What is the connection between this movie and the Korean one? I've only watched the Korean version.
  - I didn’t know there was a Korean version! Neat!
    - It even came out before the North American version!

It's very good too!
6. Na, didnt bother to watch it.
